name: artemisia vulgaris (mugwort)
intensity: mild
mechanism: Artemisia review literature discusses sesquiterpene and flavonoid pathways with anti-inflammatory and oxidative-stress modulation signals in preclinical models.
effects: Preclinical and narrative review sources describe broad pharmacologic potential across Artemisia species.
Direct human efficacy evidence specific to Artemisia vulgaris remains limited in the current curated source set.
contraindications: Avoid unsupervised use during pregnancy because uterotonic concern is repeatedly cited in traditional and safety discussions.
safetyNotes: Species-level and preparation-level variability can materially change safety profile and constituent exposure.
description: Artemisia vulgaris (mugwort) is a traditional herb in Eurasian medicine with modern pharmacology discussions concentrated in broader Artemisia reviews.
dosage: No single modern therapeutic dosage standard was established in the promoted review source.

name: catha edulis
intensity: moderate
mechanism: Primary psychoactive effects are attributed to cathinone/cathine stimulant activity on monoaminergic pathways.
effects: Human observational and review literature describes stimulant, alerting, and appetite-suppressing effects among users.
Evidence synthesis emphasizes burden and risk outcomes rather than validated therapeutic indication.
contraindications: Avoid in people with cardiovascular disease, uncontrolled hypertension, psychosis vulnerability, or substance-use instability.
safetyNotes: Systematic-review literature associates frequent use with cardiovascular, psychiatric, sleep, and oral-health concerns.
description: Catha edulis (khat) is a stimulant plant with substantial public-health literature focused on chewing prevalence and associated risks.
dosage: No safe therapeutic dosing framework is established for self-care use.

interactions: Potentially hazardous additive stimulation with sympathomimetics, caffeine-heavy products, or other psychoactive substances.
preparation: Use is most commonly fresh-leaf chewing rather than standardized medicinal dosing forms.
region: Most strongly documented in the Horn of Africa and the Arabian Peninsula, with diaspora use globally.
traditionalUse: Traditional social use (leaf chewing) is documented in East African and Arabian contexts and should remain separate from medical efficacy claims.
summary: Systematic-review evidence supports that khat chewing is widespread in some regions and linked to health and social risk patterns; this is not evidence of therapeutic efficacy.

name: catnip
intensity: mild
mechanism: Review literature discusses nepetalactones and polyphenols with potential CNS and anti-inflammatory pathway effects in experimental models.
effects: Traditional-use records describe calming and digestive-soothing applications.
Modern pharmacology evidence for catnip remains largely preclinical and does not establish definitive clinical outcomes.
contraindications: Avoid concentrated preparations during pregnancy until clearer safety evidence is available.
safetyNotes: Human safety data are limited and product composition can vary substantially by preparation.
description: Catnip (Nepeta cataria) is a Nepeta genus herb used in traditional preparations, with modern literature emphasizing phytochemistry and preclinical pharmacology.
dosage: No standardized evidence-based adult therapeutic dosing was established in the promoted source.

preparation: Most common forms are dried-leaf infusions and tinctures with variable constituent strength.
region: Native to parts of Europe and Asia and now naturalized widely, including North America.
traditionalUse: Traditional preparations include tea use for restlessness, digestive discomfort, and mild cold-related symptoms.
summary: Promoted evidence for catnip is mostly traditional-use and preclinical review data; robust modern human efficacy evidence is limited.

mechanism: CBD engages multiple receptor and enzyme pathways; mechanistic complexity and formulation variability limit simple efficacy extrapolation across products.
effects: Human evidence supports indication-specific benefit in regulated contexts rather than broad universal efficacy claims.
Adverse event and interaction monitoring remains central to risk management.
contraindications: Use caution in significant hepatic impairment without clinician oversight.
Avoid unsupported replacement of prescribed therapy with non-standardized CBD products.
safetyNotes: Monitor for liver enzyme elevations in higher-risk populations and polypharmacy contexts.
Sedation, gastrointestinal effects, and drug-drug interactions are key counseling points.
description: Cannabidiol (CBD) is a cannabinoid used in regulated products (for example prescription formulations) and consumer supplements with variable quality standards.
dosage: Dose context is product- and indication-specific; use approved labeling or clinician guidance rather than generic self-dosing ranges.

name: chamomile
intensity: mild
mechanism: Likely involves multi-component phytochemistry (for example apigenin-related GABAergic and anti-inflammatory hypotheses), but definitive clinical mechanism remains unresolved.
effects: Systematic review data suggest possible benefit for generalized anxiety symptoms in some populations.
contraindications: Avoid use in individuals with known severe ragweed/Asteraceae allergy.
safetyNotes: Generally well tolerated, but allergic reactions can occur, especially in Asteraceae-sensitive individuals.
description: Chamomile (Matricaria chamomilla/recutita) is widely used as tea and extract for calming and gastrointestinal comfort, with modern trials focused on anxiety outcomes.
dosage: Evidence is formulation-specific (tea vs extract); no single universal dosage applies across products.
duration: No direct duration data. Contextual inference: ; nan; nan.. No direct mechanismofaction data. Contextual inference: ; nan; nan.. ; nan; nan. No direct effects data. interactions: Potential additive sedation with sedative drugs; monitor anticoagulant co-use cautiously in high-intake extract contexts.
preparation: Usually prepared as infusion (tea) or standardized extract in capsules/liquids.
region: Native to Europe and Western Asia; now cultivated globally.
traditionalUse: Traditional use includes digestive and calming applications across European and Middle Eastern herbal traditions.
summary: Chamomile has human clinical review evidence suggesting potential anxiety-symptom benefit, but effect sizes and study heterogeneity warrant conservative interpretation.

name: crocus sativus
intensity: mild
mechanism: Hypothesized mechanisms include serotonergic modulation and antioxidant/anti-inflammatory signaling; mechanistic certainty remains incomplete.
effects: Meta-analysis of randomized trials reports symptom improvements versus controls in depression/anxiety contexts.
Magnitude and certainty vary by extract standardization, comparator, and trial quality.
contraindications: Use caution in pregnancy and in bipolar-spectrum conditions unless supervised by qualified clinicians.
safetyNotes: Short-term controlled use is often tolerated, but concentrated extracts can differ from culinary exposure.
description: Crocus sativus (saffron) is a culinary and medicinal spice with modern randomized-trial syntheses for mood-related outcomes.
dosage: Dose-response depends on standardized extract and indication; no single cross-product dose is appropriate.

interactions: Potential interactions with serotonergic or CNS-active medicines require medication-level review.
preparation: Used as culinary stigma material and as standardized oral extracts in clinical research.
region: Cultivated historically in Iran and the Mediterranean, now distributed globally.
traditionalUse: Traditional Persian and Mediterranean medical systems include saffron for mood and general vitality support.
summary: Recent meta-analysis evidence suggests saffron preparations may improve depression and anxiety outcomes in some settings, though formulation and trial heterogeneity remain important limitations.

name: derris elliptica
intensity: Severe (if ingested)
mechanism: Rotenone inhibits NADH dehydrogenase in the electron transport chain, disrupting ATP production and causing cellular hypoxia.
contraindications: unsafe for human consumption. do not ingest or inhale powdered forms.
safetyNotes: muscle paralysis. long-term rotenone exposure linked to parkinson’s-like symptoms.
Very high. Rotenone has been used in rodenticides and insecticides.

name: guaraná
intensity: moderate
mechanism: Mechanistic hypotheses center on caffeine and methylxanthine-mediated adenosine antagonism plus possible polyphenol contributions.
effects: Meta-analysis reports modest improvements in selected cognitive-performance domains.
Clinical effects are context-dependent and should not be generalized to all formulations.
contraindications: Use caution in people with arrhythmia risk, uncontrolled hypertension, panic disorder, or severe insomnia.
safetyNotes: Caffeine-related adverse effects (for example insomnia, palpitations, anxiety) remain a practical safety concern.
description: Guarana (Paullinia cupana) is a caffeine-rich Amazonian plant used in beverages and supplements, with modern clinical syntheses focused on cognition and fatigue.
dosage: Clinical dosing varies by caffeine-equivalent content and standardized extract composition.

name: hyoscyamus niger
intensity: Extreme
mechanism: Key alkaloids act as muscarinic antagonists. Preclinical studies characterize alkaloid variability, while clinical review literature emphasizes toxicodynamic risk.
effects: Anticholinergic manifestations (for example delirium, agitation, tachycardia, mydriasis) are reported in toxicity-focused human literature.
Alkaloid composition varies by growing conditions in preclinical compositional studies.
contraindications: Avoid in children, pregnancy, and breastfeeding due to severe toxicity potential.
Avoid in patients with glaucoma, urinary retention risk, or severe psychiatric vulnerability.
safetyNotes: High poisoning risk with unpredictable alkaloid content.
Potential for severe neuropsychiatric and autonomic adverse effects.
description: Hyoscyamus niger is a historical medicinal and ritual nightshade with modern concern centered on poisoning and anticholinergic toxidrome.
dosage: No reliable safe oral self-dosing range is established in available governed evidence.
duration: No direct duration data. Contextual inference: a notorious nightshade plant used historically in european witchcraft, sedatives, and flying ointments. contains powerful tropane alkaloids that can induce delirium, amnesia, and visionary states.. Scopolamine, hyoscyamine, and atropine block muscarinic acetylcholine receptors (anticholinergic), disrupting sensory and cognitive processing.. Hallucinations;Disorientation;Sedation;Dryness;Trance. europe, north africa, asia. ; nan; nan
interactions: Potentially dangerous when combined with sedatives, alcohol, or psychoactive substances.
legalStatus: Legal in some countries, restricted elsewhere
preparation: Historically prepared as smoked, infused, or topical ritual material; modern toxicology sources support avoidance outside medical toxicology contexts.
region: Native/introduced across parts of Europe, North Africa, and Asia.
therapeuticUses: used in minute doses for pain
or surgery pre-medication. historically employed in magic and sedation.
medication. historically employed in magic and sedation.
traditionalUse: Traditional European and West Asian use included sedative and ritual contexts, but historical use does not demonstrate modern efficacy or safety.
sideEffects: urinary retention. risk of fatal respiratory failure.
summary: Hyoscyamus niger (henbane) is a tropane-alkaloid-containing nightshade with strong anticholinergic toxicity risk; evidence supports safety warnings more than therapeutic positioning.

mechanism: A fungal symbiosis (Periglandula) is linked to ergoline alkaloid production in plant tissues and associated defensive functions.
effects: Mechanistic literature supports alkaloid-related psychoactive potential and plant defense roles.
No controlled human therapeutic efficacy evidence was established in the current promoted source set.
contraindications: Avoid in people with psychosis risk, bipolar instability, or during pregnancy/breastfeeding.
safetyNotes: Ergoline-associated exposure can be unpredictable and may carry significant neuropsychiatric risk.
description: Ipomoea tricolor (morning glory) is discussed in ethnobotanical and plant-symbiosis literature, including ergoline-associated chemistry.
dosage: No validated therapeutic dosage recommendation is supported by promoted sources.
interactions: Potential additive effects with serotonergic or other psychoactive substances.
preparation: Historical ritual contexts involve seed use; modern self-experimentation is not supported by safety evidence.
region: Native to Mesoamerica and cultivated widely as an ornamental species.
traditionalUse: Historical Mesoamerican ritual use is described in ethnobotanical records and should be treated as traditional-use context.
summary: Current promoted evidence for Ipomoea tricolor is mechanistic and ecological, not human clinical efficacy evidence.

name: mandrake
intensity: very strong
mechanism: Mandragora species contain tropane alkaloids (for example hyoscyamine/scopolamine-like compounds) that exert antimuscarinic effects; available mechanistic data are preclinical and do not establish therapeutic efficacy.
effects: Anticholinergic toxicity patterns are documented in clinical toxicology literature.
Preclinical chemistry work supports tropane-alkaloid biosynthesis variability across Mandragora species.
contraindications: Avoid in pregnancy and breastfeeding because safety has not been established and anticholinergic toxicity is plausible.
Contraindicated in people at risk for anticholinergic complications (for example narrow-angle glaucoma or urinary retention risk).
safetyNotes: Risk of delirium, tachycardia, dry mouth, urinary retention, and confusion from anticholinergic exposure.
Toxicity management requires medical supervision; non-clinical ingestion is high risk.
description: Mandrake is a Solanaceae plant historically documented in Mediterranean and Middle Eastern materia medica and ritual contexts. Modern publications focus on tropane alkaloid chemistry and poisoning management concerns.
dosage: No safe self-care dosage is established in reviewed sources; toxicology-focused literature should guide cautionary copy only.
duration: No direct duration data. Contextual inference: Used in ritual, dreaming or folk medicine.. No direct mechanismofaction data. interactions: Potential additive anticholinergic burden with other anticholinergic medicines.
Potential additive CNS effects with sedatives or alcohol; toxicity risk may increase.
preparation: Traditional preparations include roots in decoctions or topical folkloric preparations, but modern safety literature does not support unsupervised internal use.
region: Mediterranean basin and parts of Western/Central Asia in traditional records.
traditionalUse: Traditional records describe sedative, ritual, and analgesic uses; these historical uses should not be interpreted as evidence of modern clinical efficacy.
summary: Mandrake (Mandragora spp.) contains tropane alkaloids with anticholinergic activity; modern literature emphasizes toxicity risk and the need for strict safety framing rather than self-medication claims.

name: mistletoe
intensity: Moderate
mechanism: Contains viscotoxins, lectins, and alkaloids that affect immune cells, induce apoptosis, and modulate autonomic nervous system activity. Used in anthroposophic cancer therapy.
contraindications: or autoimmune conditions without supervision. toxic at high doses.
safetyNotes: or flu-like symptoms possible. oral use milder but requires caution.

sources.note: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC3201634/;European Medicines Agency;Plants of the Gods – Rätsch

description: a sacred plant in ancient druidic and european traditions, mistletoe has been used both ritually and medicinally for nervous disorders, cancer support, and hypertension. often associated with peace and protection.
dosage: Subcutaneous injection in integrative cancer therapy
interactions: may interfere with immunosuppressants or chemotherapeutics.
legalStatus: Legal but regulated in some countries
preparation: Extracts used in standardized injectable cancer therapies (e.g., Iscador). Traditional tea/tincture use with caution.
region: europe, western asia
therapeuticUses: and integrative cancer care. noted for its calming and immune-balancing effects.
and integrative cancer care. noted for its calming and immune
traditionalUse: Used in ritual, dreaming or folk medicine
sideEffects: like symptoms possible. oral use milder but requires caution.

preparation: Brewed as part of analog ayahuasca with MAOI-containing plants

safetyNotes: psychological and physiological risks if misused

name: tylophora indica
intensity: Moderate
mechanism: Contains tylophorine alkaloids with immunosuppressive, anti-inflammatory, and antiallergic properties. Reduces histamine release and cytokine activity.
contraindications: or in emaciated individuals. not for long-term high-dose use.
or in emaciated individuals. not for long
safetyNotes: nausea and vomiting in higher doses. gi discomfort is common early in treatment.
Moderate in high doses. Emetic effects limit overuse.
description: an ayurvedic herb used for bronchial conditions, immune regulation, and allergic responses. known as indian ipecac for its traditional role in treating asthma and respiratory issues.
dosage: 400 mg
day extract or 1
2 g dried leaf
legalStatus: Legal in India; regulated in some countries due to emetic effects
preparation: Leaves chewed raw, powdered for capsules, or used in low-dose decoctions. Often combined with other expectorants.
region: india, sri lanka, southeast asia
therapeuticUses: and inflammation. sometimes used topically for skin disorders.
sideEffects: nausea and vomiting in higher doses. gi discomfort is common early in treatment.

mechanism: Proposed actions include HPA-axis modulation and anti-inflammatory signaling; these mechanisms remain partly inferential across study designs.
effects: Randomized controlled trial evidence suggests improvements in stress outcomes in some adults.
contraindications: Avoid in pregnancy unless supervised by qualified clinicians.
safetyNotes: Short-term studies suggest tolerability, but real-world products vary in concentration and quality.
description: Withania somnifera is the scientific name for ashwagandha and is used in Ayurvedic practice and modern standardized supplements.
dosage: Clinical dosing is extract-specific and cannot be generalized across powders, teas, and concentrated products.
interactions: Monitor potential interactions with sedatives and thyroid-related medications.
preparation: Typically prepared as root powder or standardized root extract capsules/tablets.
region: Originates in South Asia and is now marketed worldwide.
traditionalUse: Traditional Ayurvedic use includes vitality, sleep, and stress resilience support.
summary: Withania somnifera has moderate randomized evidence for stress-related symptom improvements in standardized extracts, with unresolved long-term safety and interaction questions.
